content: Cardiometabolic syndrome (metabolic syndrome) is a cluster of interconnected risk factors increasing ASCVD and type 2 diabetes risk. Pathophysiology centers on insulin resistance, visceral adiposity, chronic inflammation, and endothelial dysfunction. Diagnostic criteria (harmonized definition): ≥3 of 5: (1) abdominal obesity (waist circumference ≥102cm men or ≥88cm women; ethnicity-specific cutoffs), (2) triglycerides ≥150 mg/dL or on treatment, (3) HDL <40 mg/dL men or <50 mg/dL women, (4) blood pressure ≥130/85 mmHg or on treatment, (5) fasting glucose ≥100 mg/dL or on treatment. Prevalence ~20-25% globally, increases with age. Associated conditions: NAFLD, PCOS, obstructive sleep apnea, hyperuricemia/gout, prothrombotic state (elevated PAI-1, fibrinogen), proinflammatory state (elevated CRP, IL-6). Risk: 2x CV events, 5x increased diabetes risk.

Metabolic syndrome management targets individual components and overall CV risk. Lifestyle modification is cornerstone: weight loss (5-10% reduces all components), Mediterranean or DASH diet, physical activity (≥150 min/week moderate aerobic plus resistance training), smoking cessation, sleep optimization, stress reduction. Pharmacologic treatment: statins for dyslipidemia (primary therapy regardless of metabolic syndrome diagnosis), antihypertensives (ACEi/ARB preferred—may improve insulin sensitivity), metformin for prediabetes/diabetes (Diabetes Prevention Program showed 31% diabetes risk reduction with lifestyle vs 58% with metformin), GLP-1 receptor agonists for obesity/diabetes (promote weight loss, CV benefit), SGLT2 inhibitors for diabetes/CVD/HF (CV and renal benefits). Antiplatelet therapy (aspirin) considered based on overall ASCVD risk. Treat NAFLD with weight loss, vitamin E in select non-diabetic patients. Address sleep apnea with CPAP. No single medication treats all components—comprehensive risk factor management essential.

Pathophysiology of metabolic syndrome: visceral adipose tissue is metabolically active, releasing free fatty acids, adipokines (leptin, resistin, visfatin), and proinflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, MCP-1). This creates chronic low-grade inflammation and insulin resistance. Free fatty acids impair insulin signaling in liver and muscle, increase hepatic glucose production, reduce glucose uptake. Adiponectin (anti-inflammatory, insulin-sensitizing) is reduced. Ectopic fat deposition in liver (NAFLD), muscle, pancreas worsens metabolic dysfunction. Endothelial dysfunction results from oxidative stress, reduced NO bioavailability, increased endothelin-1. Prothrombotic state: elevated PAI-1, fibrinogen, factor VII; impaired fibrinolysis. Mitochondrial dysfunction, gut microbiome alterations, genetic predisposition contribute. Insulin resistance precedes hyperglycemia by years—compensatory hyperinsulinemia maintains normal glucose until beta-cell failure. Early intervention critical before irreversible organ damage.
